sanskrit_devanagari:
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिरेकेह कुरुनन्दन ।
बहुशाखा ह्यनन्ताश्च बुद्धयोऽव्यवसायिनाम् ॥ ४१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekeha kuru-nandana |
bahu-śākhā hy anantāś ca buddhayo 'vyavasāyinām || 41 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact body text. Vocative rendered as कुरूनन्दन (long ū) per vedabase house style for sandhi-lengthened compound boundary; gitasupersite, holy-bhagavad-gita and GRETIL render the short-u form कुरुनन्दन (kurunandana). The akṣara sequence of meaning is identical; this is an orthographic / sandhi-rendering variant only.
exact (short-u वocative कुरुनन्दन; single danda terminator; body text identical otherwise)
exact (short-u vocative कुरुनन्दन; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.41।।; body text identical)
IAST exact (vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekeha kuru-nandana / bahu-śākhā hy anantāś ca buddhayo 'vyavasāyinām)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.041 = MBh_06,026.041 in critical-edition numbering). IAST body identical: 'vyavasāyātmikā buddhir ekeha kurunandana / bahuśākhā hy anantāś ca buddhayo \u2019vyavasāyinām'. GRETIL writes the compound 'kurunandana' as a single token with short u, agreeing with gitasupersite and holy-bhagavad-gita.

anvaya.meaning: of the nature of resolution / one-pointed in determination
O joy of the Kurus (vocative addressing Arjuna)
of the irresolute / of those without determination

translations.verbatim_quote: Those who are on this path are resolute in purpose, and their aim is one. O beloved child of the Kurus, the intelligence of those who are irresolute is many-branched.
O descendent of the Kurus, the intellect of those who are on this path is resolute, and their aim is one-pointed. But the intellect of those who are irresolute is many-branched.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: A strong faith that by Kṛṣṇa consciousness one will be elevated to the highest perfection of life is called vyavasāyātmikā intelligence. Faith means unflinching trust in something sublime. When one is engaged in the duties of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, he need not act in relationship to the material world with obligations to family traditions, humanity, or nationality.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.41 Kuru-nandana, O scion of the Kuru dynasty; iha, is this path to Liberation; there is only eka, a single; vyavasayatmika, one-pointed; buddhih, conviction, which has been spoken of in the Yoga of Knowledge and which has the characteristics going to be spoken of in (Karma-) yoga. It is resolute by nature and annuls the numerous branches of the other opposite thoughts.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads vyavasāyātmikā buddhi as the single one-pointed conviction shared by jñāna-niṣṭha (Yoga of Knowledge) and karma-yoga: it is resolute because it derives from a right pramāṇa (the Vedic śabda properly understood). The 'many-branched' intelligences are the outputs of those who lack this anchoring — kāmya-karma fixations whose proliferation is itself diagnostic of irresolution. Crucially, Shankara does NOT oppose the Vedas; he opposes the unsteady reading that fixates on the kāmya portions and proliferates branches.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.41 Here, i.e., in every ritual sanctioned by the scriptures, the Buddhi or disposition of mind marked by resolution, is single. The Buddhi marked by resolution is the Buddhi concerned with acts which must be performed by one desirous of release (and not any kind of work). The term 'Vyavasaya' means unshakable conviction: this Buddhi (disposition of mind) comes out of prior determination about the true nature of the self.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads vyavasāya as the unshakable conviction that arises from prior determination about the true nature of the self (ātma-yāthātmya). The single resolute Buddhi is the one concerned with acts whose performance leads to release (mokṣa-aṅga). The many-branched Buddhi is the disposition that engages rituals only for fruit-collection (svarga, opulence). Both Buddhis read the Vedas; the difference is in what is sought through them. Ramanuja therefore agrees with Shankara on the diagnosis but frames the corrective in Vishishtadvaita terms (devotional release, not undifferentiated Knowledge).

positions.rendering: resolute (in Kṛṣṇa consciousness) intelligence; aim is one
single one-pointed conviction grounded in right pramāṇa, shared by jñāna-niṣṭha and karma-yoga
unshakable conviction arising from prior determination of the true nature of the self; concerned with mokṣa-aṅga acts only

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree that vyavasāyātmikā buddhi is the resolute, one-pointed determination — the operative term is vyavasāya (resolution / decision-after-deliberation, not impulse). The disagreement is doctrinal scope. Prabhupada scopes resolution to Kṛṣṇa-consciousness specifically; Mukundananda flattens to a tradition-neutral 'one-pointed intellect.' Shankara reads it as the shared conviction of jñāna and karma-yoga grounded in correct pramāṇa; Ramanuja reads it as the conviction arising from ātma-yāthātmya and oriented toward acts that lead to release. The verse therefore is not 'have any resolute opinion'; it is 'have the resolution that follows from a correctly grounded determination about the substrate.' This scoping matters: 'any decision' is not what vyavasāya names.

literal_meaning: Here, O joy of the Kurus, the resolute intelligence is one; the intelligences of the irresolute are many-branched and indeed endless.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a state of intelligence — vyavasāyātmikā buddhi — as the precondition for the buddhi-yoga he is about to lay out. The resolute intelligence is one-pointed because it has been grounded in a prior determination about what is real / what the substrate is / what action produces release. The irresolute proliferate branches: they entertain endless variant proposals because they have not done the prior grounding. Both Shankara and Ramanuja read 'iha' (here) as scoping the verse to the path of action-without-attachment that has been introduced (2.39 onward) and is being elaborated. The verse is therefore the doorway into buddhi-yoga: only the one-pointed buddhi can practice it. Importantly, the multi-branchedness of the irresolute is named as a state of intelligence, not a strategy — entertaining many proposals to defer commitment is itself the diagnostic of avyavasāya.

remaining_gaps: Madhva's Brahma-Sampradaya commentary not pulled — would require separate source.
